skill.name: browser-automation
skill.description: Browser automation powers web testing, scraping, and AI agent interactions. The difference between a flaky script and a reliable system comes down to understanding selectors, waiting strategies, and detection patterns.
skill.summary: Master reliable browser automation with Playwright patterns for testing and scraping.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced false positives on markdown content. Backticks on line 71 are inline code formatting, not shell execution. No cryptographic code exists on lines 3 or 74. This is purely instructional documentation with no executable security risks.

content.value_statement: Flaky browser tests waste hours of debugging time. Learn Playwright patterns for stable selectors, smart waits, and detection-resistant automation.
actual_capabilities: Design stable element selectors using user-facing locators
Implement auto-wait patterns to eliminate flaky timing issues
Build isolated test architectures with fresh state per test
Create detection-resistant scrapers with stealth techniques
Configure proper viewport and tracing for debugging
limitations: Does not provide executable code, only patterns and guidance
Requires Playwright or Puppeteer as the automation framework
Does not cover mobile browser automation specifics

use_cases.title: E2E Test Architecture
use_cases.description: Build reliable end-to-end tests that run consistently in CI/CD pipelines without flaky failures.
use_cases.target_user: QA engineers and test automation developers

use_cases.title: Web Scraping Systems
use_cases.description: Create scrapers that handle dynamic content, avoid detection, and run long-term without breaking.
use_cases.target_user: Data engineers and researchers collecting web data

use_cases.title: AI Agent Browser Interaction
use_cases.description: Enable AI agents to interact with web interfaces reliably using proper wait strategies and selectors.
use_cases.target_user: AI developers building browser-using autonomous agents

prompt_templates.title: Beginner: Fix Flaky Test Selector
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a flaky test that fails when trying to click a button. The test uses CSS selectors but sometimes the element is not found. Help me rewrite it using user-facing locators and proper wait patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Intermediate: Build Test Isolation
prompt_templates.prompt: My tests are interfering with each other because they share browser state. Show me how to structure my test suite so each test runs in complete isolation with fresh cookies, storage, and context.
prompt_templates.scenario: Refactoring a test suite for reliability

prompt_templates.title: Advanced: Anti-Detection Scraping
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to scrape a website that uses bot detection. Guide me through implementing stealth techniques, proper delays between requests, and handling popup dialogs that appear unexpectedly.

prompt_templates.title: Expert: Debug Test Failure with Traces
prompt_templates.prompt: My test is failing intermittently in CI but passes locally. Help me configure trace collection, set consistent viewport settings, and analyze the failure to identify the root cause.

output_examples.input: Test fails with element not found error on login button
output_examples.output: Replace CSS selector with getByRole: await page.getByRole('button', { name: 'Sign in' }).click(). Playwright auto-waits for the element to be actionable.

output_examples.input: Tests pass individually but fail when run together
output_examples.output: Create fresh browser context per test using test.beforeEach with context = await browser.newContext(). This isolates cookies and localStorage between tests.

best_practices: Use user-facing locators like getByRole, getByText, and getByLabel instead of fragile CSS or XPath selectors
Trust Playwright's auto-wait mechanism rather than adding manual timeouts or sleep calls
Run each test in a fresh browser context to ensure complete isolation and prevent state leakage
anti_patterns: Adding arbitrary waitForTimeout calls instead of waiting for specific conditions
Using CSS selectors or XPath as the first choice instead of semantic user-facing locators
Sharing a single browser context across multiple tests which causes state pollution

faq.question: Why do my tests fail intermittently in CI but pass locally?
faq.answer: CI environments have different timing characteristics. Use proper waits for elements to be actionable, enable trace collection for debugging, and ensure each test has complete isolation.

faq.question: What is the difference between CSS selectors and user-facing locators?
faq.answer: User-facing locators like getByRole reflect how users interact with your app and are more resilient to UI changes. CSS selectors couple tests to implementation details that may change during refactoring.

faq.question: How do I make my scraper avoid bot detection?
faq.answer: Add realistic delays between requests, use stealth plugins to mask automation signatures, rotate user agents, and mimic human-like interaction patterns with mouse movements.

faq.question: Should I use Playwright or Puppeteer for my project?
faq.answer: Playwright supports multiple browsers and has built-in auto-wait. Puppeteer is Chrome-focused but lighter. Choose Playwright for cross-browser testing, Puppeteer for Chrome-only needs.

faq.question: How do I handle popup dialogs that appear during tests?
faq.answer: Wait for the popup dialog to appear before triggering the action that opens it. Use page.on('dialog') event handlers or waitForEvent to catch and handle dialogs properly.

faq.question: What viewport size should I use for testing?
faq.answer: Set a consistent viewport size across all tests using browser.newContext({ viewport: { width: 1280, height: 720 } }). This ensures reproducible layouts and screenshot comparisons.
